question: In Zamharir 2015, what relative density was achieved for ZrB2-25vol% SiC at the optimal hot pressing conditions of 1850°C, 90 min holding time, 16 MPa pressure, and 200 nm SiC particle size?
reference_answer: A relative density of approximately 99.6% was achieved for the ZrB2-25vol% SiC composite at these optimal hot pressing conditions. The confirmation test conducted at these parameters produced five measurements ranging from 99.3% to 99.8% relative density, with an average of 99.60%, confirming the effectiveness of this parameter combination.
evidence_any: temperature of 1850 1C, holding time of 90 min, pressure of 16 MPa and SiC particle size of 200 nm
confirmation test: outcomes of measured relative density (%) 99.3 99.5 99.6 99.8 99.8 99.60
excerpts: According to ANOVA, the optimal process conditions are the temperature of 1850 1C, the holding time of 90 min, the applied pressure of 16MPa and the SiC particle size of 200 nm. [Table 7 ](#8)indicates the expected content of the relative density for the optimal processing conditions and results of the confirmation test applied at the optimal qualification. As shown in [Table 7](#8), the relative density at the optimal processing conditions is 100.459, which indicates that ZrB2–SiC compo- site has reached full density. The confirmation test is a critical and definite step in establishing the outcomes attained from the Taguchi method. Indeed, accomplishment of the confirmation test is necessary to obtain the optimal conditions via compar- ing the measured results with the expected consequences. The approximation is superlatively associated with the actual relative density of the samples densified at optimal conditions. In other words, the experimental consequences accord the initial design and analysis for optimizing the processing factors.

Table 7 Optimum conditions and performance for relative density of hot pressed ZrB2– SiC composite. Factor Level description Level Contribution (%) Temperature (1C) 1850 3 4.577 Holding time (min) 90 3 2.844 Applied pressure (MPa) 16 3 5.111 Particle size of SiC 200 2 0.897 Total contribution from all factors 13.429 Current grand average of performance 87.03 Expected result at optimum condition 100.459 Confirmation test: outcomes of measured relative density (%) 99.3 99.5 99.6 99.8 99.8 99.60

question: Why did Zamharir 2015 add ultrafine SiC particles to the ZrB2 matrix for the densification of this ceramic composite?
reference_answer: Ultrafine SiC particles were added to enhance the sinterability of ZrB2, inhibit grain coarsening, improve mechanical properties, and enhance oxidation/reduction resistance. ZrB2 has poor sinterability due to its covalent bonding and low bulk and grain boundary diffusion rates, which prevent it from being used in many applications, making the addition of SiC necessary to improve these properties.
evidence_any: Its poor sinterability prevents this material from being used in a wide range of applications
the addition of ultrafine SiC to the ZrB2 matrix is performed to enhance sinterability
excerpts: Generally densification of a single-phase ZrB2 is difficult and usually needs high temperatures because of its covalent bonding and low bulk and grain boundary diffusion rates. Its poor sinterability prevents this material from being used in a wide range of applications; therefore sinterability of ZrB2 must be improved before the potential applications of ZrB2 can be fully realized. The introduction of secondary phases or sintering aids has been an appropriate strategy for improving the sinterability of monolithic ZrB2 ceramics.

Generally, the addition of ultrafine SiC to the ZrB2 matrix is performed to enhance sinterability, inhibit the grain coarsening, improve mechanical properties, and enhance oxidation/reduction resistance. Hence, introducing SiC particles into ZrB2 yields a ZrB2–SiC ceramic composite that is more sinterable than the monolithic ZrB2.

question: In Zamharil 2015, why did the ZrB2-25vol% SiC composite sintered at 1700°C show particle fragmentation and rearrangement as the dominant densification mechanisms?
reference_answer: At the low temperature of 1700°C, the ZrB2 particles fragmented and rearranged without significant chemical bonding or sintering. This occurred because ZrB2 has low volume and grain boundary diffusion rates due to its covalent bonding nature, which requires higher temperatures to activate plastic deformation and grain boundary diffusion mechanisms necessary for proper sintering.
evidence_any: specimens were heated from room temperature to 1000 1C at a rate of 17 1C/min
excerpts: The sintering process was conducted in vacuum (5 10 2 Pa). At first, specimens were heated from room temperature to 1000 1C at a rate of 17 1C/min. To facilitate the removal of oxide impurities from the samples, the heating cycle included isothermal hold of 20 min at 1000 1C, followed by heating at 17 1C/min up to the delineated temperature. For the temperatures above 1000 1C, the temperature of graphite die was monitored with an infrared temperature sensor (Dikai IT6, China) through a sapphire window. The hot pressing process was operated at different temperatures (1700, 1775 or 1850 1C), given a dwell isotherm for 30, 60 or 90 min. In all of the hot pressing runs, with starting the final isothermal temperature cycle, an external mechanical load of 8, 12 or 16 MPa was applied.

After seeing the low relative density of T-1 (78.1%) and the respective microstructure after hot pressing at the cited conditions, ZrB2 particles fragmentation and rearrangement were recognized as the main densification mechanisms. At the low temperature of 1700 1C, the dominant mechanisms were mechanical interweaving, particles fragmentation and rearrangement, without any significant chemical bonding. Moreover, as it can be seen in [Fig. 3](#5)b, the shape of the raw materials did not change, which demonstrates the sintering process did not occur completely. Therefore, the initial densification mechanisms in hot pressing of ceramic composites were particle fragmentation and rearrangement. Other densification mechanisms such as plastic deformation of grains and grain boundary diffusion were activated at higher temperatures.

question: In Zamharir 2015, why did trial T-8 with 20 nm SiC particles achieve lower density (86.64%) compared to trial T-7 with 200 nm SiC particles (96.5%), despite having finer particles?
reference_answer: The lower density in T-8 was caused by agglomeration of the nano-sized 20 nm SiC particles combined with the low applied pressure of 8 MPa. The high activity and surface area of nano-sized SiC enhanced the tendency for agglomeration, which increased closed porosity at agglomeration sites and inhibited ZrB2 grain growth at triple points.
evidence_any: the relative density of T-7 (96.5%) is higher than T-8 (86.64%)
excerpts: In some studies [22](#9),[23\]](#9), it has been reported that the densification enhances with decreasing the particle size of SiC. However, here, with seeing the values of the relative density in [Table 5](#5), despite the particle size of SiC in T-8 (20 nm) is finer than T-7 (200 nm), the relative density of T-7 (96.5%) is higher than T-8 (86.64%). This observation can relate to the agglomeration of nano-sized SiC particles (20 nm) as well as the low applied pressure (8 MPa), which causes the lower density of the sample.

High activity and surface area of nano-sized SiC enhances the tendency of agglomeration, therefore porosity (closed pores) is increased in agglomeration sites. Microstructural analysis shows the SiC agglomerates, normally located at the triple point of the ZrB2 grains ([Fig. 5](#6)), cause the inhibition of the ZrB2 grain growth.

question: In Zamharir 2015, what was the role of glassy borosilicate phases observed in the microstructure of trial T-7 sintered at 1850°C with 200 nm SiC particles?
reference_answer: The glassy borosilicate phases formed from the oxidized surface layer of nano-sized SiC particles promoted densification by covering the ZrB2 particles like bedsheets. These glassy phases likely acted as a sintering aid by providing a liquid-like pathway for atomic diffusion at the elevated sintering temperature, enhancing the densification process.
evidence_any: glassy phases are obviously observable in [Fig. 4 ](#5)(marked by the arrows), which have covered the ZrB2 particles like bedsheets
excerpts: Microstructure of fracture surface of T-7, hot pressed at 1850 1C for 30 min under 16 MPa with SiC particle size of 200 nm, is shown in [Fig. 4](#5). As indicated in the SEM micrograph, neck formation between particles was observed in the sample, which relieved the promotion of the densification. Although neck formation is a sign of diffusion-controlled sintering, but it seems that applying higher external pressures at elevated temperatures can cause the plastic deformation of ZrB2 grains. Some deformed ZrB2 grains can be distinguished by their smooth polygon faces (marked by the arrows in [Fig. 4](#5)). In addition, a compact network of dislocations inside ZrB2 grains, which was detected in the hot pressed ZrB2–SiC composites at 1850 1C, can also help the plastic deformation of ZrB2 grains due to the applied pressure.

As it can be seen in T-7 and based on other Refs. [17](#9),[27\]](#9), it was found that the addition of nano-sized SiC particles in ZrB2-based ceramic composites, can cause the formation of slight intergranular liquid phase during the hot pressing process, which can assist the densification process. The beneficial influence of nano-sized SiC on the sintering process of ZrB2-based composites can be due to the fact that most of nano-sized SiC particles have an oxidized surface layer, which promotes the formation of rough borosilicate glassy phases. In general, a glassy phase can be detected in SEM micrographs by its visual feature such as planar or cleavage state, thin thickness or brittle and smooth fracture surface. Such glassy phases are obviously observable in [Fig. 4 ](#5)(marked by the arrows), which have covered the ZrB2 particles like bedsheets.

question: In Rizzi 2024, what was the particle size range of TiC-TiAl powder after self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS), and why was milling necessary before spark plasma sintering?
reference_answer: After SHS synthesis, the TiC-TiAl powder particles were in the order of several tens of micrometers, which was unsuitable for sintering. Milling in isopropanol for at least 4 hours was necessary to reduce the particle size to less than 5 micrometers, which is the appropriate size for spark plasma sintering (SPS) consolidation.
evidence_any: The particle size after SHS is unsuitable for sintering as they are in the order of several tens of micrometers
excerpts: The SHS method allowed the synthesis of a large amount of powder in a very short time and an efficient conversion rate due to the elevated temperatures reached because of the high exothermicity of these re- actions and the accumulation of heat. After the SHS, the powder is constituted by a mixture of TiC, TiAl, TiC powder was obtained by reaction of Ti and graphite while the composite TiC–TiAl was obtained by adding Al powder in such a con- centration as to obtain 5 vol%, 10 vol% and 20 vol% of TiAl according to the stoichiometric reaction. It is important to mention that the adiabatic temperature of combustion for TiC and for TiAl is 3209 K and 1518 K, respectively. The powder obtained by SHS was then attrition milled in isopropanol using a nylon-coated jar and rotor arms and Y-TZP balls with 5 mm in diameter as milling medium.

The particle size after SHS is unsuitable for sintering as they are in the order of several tens of micrometers (see the SEM micrographs in Fig. 2). Fig. 3 reports particle size evolution during milling in iso- propanol. The particle size substantially decreases with the milling time for all the compositions. However, it scales with the TiAl doping level (the higher the doping level the larger the particles after milling). In all the cases, the final particle size is < 5 μm, and at least 4 h were needed to mill the powder before SPS.

question: In Rizzi 2024, what were the SPS sintering conditions used for TiC with 20 vol% TiAl, and what relative density was achieved at 1350°C?
reference_answer: For TiC with 20 vol% TiAl (TiC20TiAl), spark plasma sintering was performed at 1350°C with a heating rate of 100°C/min, external pressure of 80 MPa, and holding time of 5 minutes, achieving approximately 97% relative density. This temperature was well below the melting point of the TiAly sintering aid (1460°C).
evidence_any: most of the sintering is accomplished at 1350 °C(≈97 % relative density)
excerpts: TiC–TiAl powders were used to obtain a cylinder with a diameter of 20 mm and a height of 6 mm. The samples were sintered by spark plasma sintering (SPS) using a Dr. Sinter SPS-1050-CE (Japan) furnace, in which the powders were loaded in 20 mm graphite dies, lined with graphite foils, and covered by graphite blankets. The temperature was monitored through an optical pyrometer, which works in the infrared, starting from 570 ◦C. The sintering experiments were carried out using 100 ◦C/min as the heating rate, a dwell at high temperature of 5 min and applying an external pressure of 80 MPa. The sintering temperatures were modified for each composition to achieve the highest possible densification (Table 1).

Fig. 5 reports the relative bulk density evolution for samples sintered at different temperatures. We can observe a monotonical increase in the sample density with the sintering temperature. It is quite surprising to observe that the sample containing 20 vol% of TiAl already achieved relatively good densification at only 1150 ◦C (≈85 % relative density) and most of the sintering is accomplished at 1350 ◦C(≈97 % relative density). Such temperature is well below the melting point of the aluminide phase.

question: In Rizzi 2024, how did the aluminum content in TiC20TiAl evolve during SPS sintering, and what mechanism did this enable for densification?
reference_answer: The aluminum content in TiC20TiAl decreased significantly with increasing sintering temperature, dropping to about one-fifth of its starting value after firing at 1700°C. This aluminum loss allowed the formation of a continuous grain boundary phase of TiAly that promoted sintering by enabling Ti from TiC to dissolve at the neck center and precipitate at the neck surface, while carbon diffused through the TiC lattice.
evidence_any: the Al content decreases increasing the firing temperature, thus pointing out an evolution in the material composition
excerpts: Fig. 6 reports the Al content detected by EDXS on the sintered TiC20TiAl samples as a function of the SPS temperature. We can observe that the Al content decreases increasing the firing temperature, thus pointing out an evolution in the material composition. After firing at 1700 ◦C, the Al content is about 1/5 of the starting one. Such evolution in the composition was accounted for in the calculation of the theoret- ical density used to estimate the relative density in Fig. 5.

The presence of the continuous aluminide phase at the grain boundary could allow the dissolution of Ti from the TiC crystal at the neck center, its fast diffusion through the aluminide, and its pre- cipitation at the neck surface. This is facilitated by the fact that Ti can easily dissolve inside the Ti-aluminide where lattice sites matching the Ti radius and charge are available. The suggested mechanism is sub- stantially analogous to what was observed in liquid phase sintering, with the major difference that the fast diffusion avenue is not in the liquid but in the solid state (i.e., at 1350 ◦C we are still below the melting point of the aluminide).

question: In Rizzi 2024, how did the microstructure of TiC20TiAl change from 1150°C to 1700°C during SPS, and what caused the aluminum-rich phase to form spherical clusters?
reference_answer: At 1150°C, the aluminum-rich phase was dispersed in porosity between TiC grains with irregular particle shapes. At 1350°C, it formed a continuous phase at grain boundaries and triple points. At 1700°C, the aluminum-rich phase collapsed into spherical clusters due to Rayleigh instability of the percolative film and conversion to more refractory alumina (Al2O3) through reaction with oxygen impurities.
evidence_any: At 1350 °C, the aluminum-rich phase is located at the grain boundaries and at the triple points. Finally, at 1700 °C the aluminum-rich phase collapses in spherical clusters
excerpts: The microstructural evolution at different sintering temperatures for TiC20TiAl is reported in Fig. 7. The micrographs refer to polished samples and are substantially consistent with the density data reported in Fig. 5. The microstructures are clearly biphasic, featuring a darker Al- rich phase and a brighter compound which is the TiC. Already at 1150 ◦C, the material is substantially consolidated with indeed some residual porosity. Despite some sintering happening at 1150 ◦C (density ≈85 %), the shape of the TiC particles is still irregular and characterized by sharp edges. At 1350 ◦C, the aluminum-rich phase is located at the grain boundaries between the TiC particles and at the triple points. The microstructure is nearly fully dense. At 1550 ◦C, the amount of dark (al- rich) phase is substantially reduced, but a thin layer of it can still be observed at the grain boundary and triple points. Finally, at 1700 ◦C the aluminum-rich phase collapses in spherical clusters.

Such a result is of some interest because TiAly can act as a very effective sintering additive, but in the final microstructures, the alumi- nide nearly completely disappears as it is extruded out of the die and also partially converted to alumina (see the XRD in Fig. 11) which is indeed more refractory than TiAly (Tm,TiAl = 1460 ◦C, Tm,Al2O3 = 2051 ◦C). The alumina formation can be explained based on the high af- finity between Al and O contamination in the SPS atmosphere. More- over, Alumina segregates in closed and isolated pockets at the end of the sintering process due to the Rayleigh instability of the percolative film.

question: In Rizzi 2024, how did the Vickers hardness of TiC doped with different TiAl contents (5, 10, and 20 vol%) compare, and what explained these differences?
reference_answer: The Vickers hardness of TiC specimens decreased as the amount of TiAl sintering aid increased, with higher TiAl content resulting in lower hardness values. This reduction was explained by the fact that the TiAl dopant possesses lower hardness than pure TiC, which reduced the overall hardness of the composite despite achieving high densification.
evidence_any: the hardness of the samples decreases increasing the amount of sintering aids
excerpts: A comparison of the Vickers hardness of the TiC specimens doped with different amounts of TiAl (5 vol%, 10 vol% and 20 vol%) is reported in Fig. 12, in which the Vickers hardness, measured with a load of 0.2 kg, is highlighted as a function of the relative density. In general, the hardness of the samples decreases increasing the amount of sintering aids.

The Vickers hardness (Fig. 12) decreases when increasing the initial doping content, in accordance with the ones reported in the literature. This can be attributed to the fact that the dopant possesses a hardness lower than the TiC.
